Browse fresh seafood, local produce, and street food at Jeju's oldest and most vibrant market in the city center.
Explore artifacts and exhibits showcasing Jeju's unique history, culture, and natural heritage from ancient times to the modern era.
Visit this quirky sculpture park featuring adult-themed art installations and interactive exhibits celebrating human relationships.
Walk through a preserved traditional village with thatched-roof houses, folk museums, and artisan workshops showcasing old Jeju life.
Start hiking Korea's tallest mountain from accessible trailheads near Jeju City, offering views and alpine flora.
Learn about Jeju's famous mandarin oranges (tangerines) through exhibits, tastings, and interactive displays.
Experience a large marine aquarium featuring tropical fish, sea turtles, and interactive touch pools suitable for families.

food.detail: Jeju's signature grilled pork belly sourced from the island's black pigs, known for tender meat and rich flavor.
Fresh sea urchin and abalone harvested by haenyeo, often served raw or in porridge as a local delicacy.
Sweet, seedless citrus fruit grown abundantly on the island, enjoyed fresh or in juices and desserts.
Local silverfish braised with soy, garlic, and vegetables, a comfort food widely served in Jeju City restaurants.
Chewy noodles in a hearty broth topped with shredded beef and vegetables, a traditional Jeju noodle specialty.
Spicy stew loaded with fresh local catch including octopus, clams, and shrimp, reflecting Jeju's coastal bounty.

culture.detail: Show respect to female divers and their heritage by supporting haenyeo-associated businesses and learning about their practices rather than treating them as tourist curiosities.
Jeju experiences significant seasonal changes and sudden weather shifts; check forecasts and dress in layers, especially for mountain activities.
While card payments are common, some traditional markets, street vendors, and smaller restaurants prefer or only accept cash, so carry won.
When visiting temples or natural sacred sites, dress modestly, speak quietly, and follow posted guidelines to respect local spiritual customs.

places.name: Cheonjeyeon Falls
places.description: A stunning three-tiered waterfall nestled in a lush valley, creating an enchanting natural pool where swimming is permitted in summer. The scenic hiking trail through dense forest offers an serene escape from crowds.

activities.detail: Cool off in the crystal-clear emerald pool at the base of the first tier during summer months when swimming is officially permitted.
Hike the well-maintained trail to observe all three cascading tiers of the waterfall, with each level offering distinct perspectives and photo opportunities.
Stroll slowly through the dense subtropical forest canopy surrounding the falls to experience the therapeutic benefits of being immersed in nature.
Arrive early morning to capture the waterfall with soft golden light filtering through the forest and fewer crowds present.
Walk along the lower basin where still water creates mirror-like reflections of the falls and surrounding cliff faces.
Bring local snacks and enjoy a meal on designated rest areas while listening to the soothing sound of cascading water.

food.detail: Sample grilled black pork unique to Jeju island at nearby restaurants, known for its tender texture and rich flavor.
Try this creamy local specialty made with fresh abalone from Jeju's waters, often available at restaurants near the falls.
Purchase fresh Jeju citrus fruits from local vendors at the entrance, perfect for refreshment after your hike.
Enjoy this Jeju noodle soup served with tender meat broth, a warming traditional dish popular with hikers.

culture.detail: Swimming is only permitted during designated summer months (typically June-August); outside these dates the water is dangerous and entry is prohibited.
Jeju culture emphasizes harmony with nature, so pack out all trash and stay on marked trails to preserve this sacred natural site.
Visit early morning (before 10 AM) to avoid peak tourist crowds and experience the serene atmosphere the falls are known for.
The forest trail can be slippery year-round due to moisture; locals wear sturdy hiking shoes rather than casual sneakers.

places.name: Haenyeo Diving Villages
places.description: Experience the UNESCO-recognized tradition of female free divers who have harvested seafood for centuries using only breath-holding techniques. Visitors can watch performances and learn about this unique cultural heritage in villages like Gujwa.

activities.detail: Observe skilled female divers demonstrate breath-hold diving techniques and seafood harvesting in designated performance areas during seasonal schedules.
Learn about the history, traditions, and daily lives of haenyeo through exhibits, photographs, and interactive displays dedicated to this UNESCO-recognized practice.
Explore artifacts, traditional diving gear, and personal stories of haenyeo divers spanning centuries of cultural heritage in Jeju.
Stroll along coastal areas of Gujwa village where you may encounter haenyeo preparing equipment or returning with their daily catch during morning or afternoon dives.
Join hands-on sessions where visitors learn basic diving techniques, traditional knot-tying, or seafood preparation methods from experienced haenyeo instructors.
Document the authentic daily life, fishing boats, and seaside architecture of traditional haenyeo communities with a local guide.

food.detail: Enjoy locally harvested sea urchin gathered by haenyeo divers, typically served raw with rice or in traditional bowls as a delicacy.
Savor tender abalone hand-picked by female divers and simmered in a savory broth, a signature dish of coastal Jeju villages.
Sample a variety of freshly caught seafood including octopus, clams, and seaweed prepared in multiple ways at local haenyeo restaurants.
Taste locally harvested seaweed varieties collected by divers, served as side dishes, soups, or dried snacks at village eateries.
Try fresh octopus caught by haenyeo and stir-fried with gochugaru and vegetables, a beloved Jeju coastal preparation.

culture.detail: Haenyeo typically dive during specific seasons and times of day; check local schedules and maintain safe distances from active diving areas to protect divers.
Show respect to divers by avoiding loud noises near coastal areas, not touching their equipment, and asking permission before photographing performances or individuals.
Purchase seafood and products directly from haenyeo cooperatives and local vendors to contribute meaningfully to the preservation of this endangered tradition.
Understand that haenyeo diving is traditionally female-dominated; the practice carries deep cultural and historical significance tied to women's roles in Jeju society.

places.name: Jeolmul Natural Forest
places.description: A pristine natural forest area on Hallasan's northern slope featuring native trees and well-maintained hiking trails perfect for outdoor enthusiasts. The forest provides respite with clean air and biodiversity away from tourist crowds.

activities.detail: A moderate 4.2 km hiking path that ascends through native broadleaf and conifer forests with panoramic views of Hallasan's northern slope and surrounding valleys.
Guided slow-paced walking meditation through dense groves of Jeju native trees including Korean fir and Korean pine to experience forest therapy and air quality benefits.
A scenic 2 km walk to a seasonal waterfall nestled within the forest that flows down rocky terrain, creating a peaceful natural swimming and photo opportunity during summer months.
Self-guided or ranger-led tour identifying endemic and native Jeju flora including rare species of Korean orchids, mountain azaleas, and ancient specimen trees along marked botanical zones.
Stay in designated forest cabins or campsites to experience nocturnal forest sounds and observe bioluminescent insects while sleeping surrounded by protective tree canopy.
Trek to elevated clearings before dawn to capture mist-covered forest canopy and morning light filtering through native trees, a favorite location for landscape photographers.

food.detail: A Jeju specialty rice bowl topped with fresh foraged mountain greens, mushrooms, and herbs collected from forest areas, served with spicy gochujang sauce at nearby lodge restaurants.
Locally-raised Jeju black pork cooked over charcoal at forest lodge restaurants, prized for its tender texture and rich flavor unique to the island's pork breed.
A traditional Korean herbal tea brewed from fresh or dried needles of Korean pine trees found throughout the forest, known for vitamin C content and forest aroma.
A warming broth featuring wild mushrooms foraged from Hallasan's slopes including shiitake and oyster varieties, commonly served at forest resort dining areas.
Fresh or preserved Jeju mandarin oranges available at forest entrance shops, reflecting the island's agricultural heritage and providing natural energy for hikers.

culture.detail: Hallasan is considered sacred in Korean shamanic tradition; stay on marked trails, avoid loud noise, and leave no offerings or trash as part of forest preservation respect.
Some upper trails close during winter months due to heavy snow and ice; check with the forest office before visiting to confirm accessibility and bring appropriate seasonal gear.
Locals and experienced hikers typically arrive before 8 AM to experience quieter forest conditions, better wildlife observation, and cooler temperatures during summer.
Forest lodges operate on communal dining schedules; confirm meal times upon arrival and remove shoes before entering shared spaces, following traditional Korean accommodation customs.

places.name: Gwandeok Shrine
places.description: A historic 15th-century shrine in central Jeju City representing important Jeju cultural heritage and architectural style. The shrine provides insight into local history and serves as a peaceful cultural landmark.

activities.detail: Walk through the restored 15th-century wooden structure to observe traditional Jeju architectural details and the altar dedicated to the guardian deity.
Capture images of the distinctive curved eaves and decorative roof ornaments that exemplify Joseon-era craftsmanship.
Participate in or observe traditional Confucian ceremonies held during spring and autumn equinoxes when locals honor local deities and ancestors.
Learn about Gwandeok Shrine's historical significance and Jeju's religious practices through displayed artifacts and informational panels.
Walk the peaceful courtyard and surrounding garden area to appreciate the serene atmosphere away from central Jeju City's bustle.

food.detail: Try this local millet rice cake often sold by vendors near the shrine, traditionally made with barley and served with red bean filling.
Sample grilled black pork at nearby restaurants in Jeju City, a signature local protein known for its tender quality and rich flavor.
Seek out fresh seafood dishes at markets near the shrine, reflecting Jeju's strong marine food culture.
Enjoy traditional barley tea served at nearby tea houses, a refreshing local beverage popular with visitors exploring cultural sites.

culture.detail: Wear modest, comfortable clothing when visiting the shrine, as it remains an active space for religious observance and ceremonies.
Bow before passing through the shrine gate and avoid loud conversation to honor the sacred space and other visitors.
The shrine represents Jeju's unique blend of Confucian and indigenous shamanic traditions, making it central to understanding the island's cultural identity.

places.name: Oedolgae Rock
places.description: An iconic solitary rock formation rising dramatically from the sea near Jungmun Beach, steeped in local legend and mythology. The scenic viewpoint offers excellent photography opportunities and represents Jeju's unique coastal geology.

activities.detail: Capture the iconic solitary rock formation from multiple angles, especially during golden hour when the lighting enhances the dramatic coastal landscape.
Stroll along the scenic pathway connecting Oedolgae viewpoint to nearby Jungmun Beach, enjoying coastal views and access to local shops.
Learn the tragic mythological story of the rock formation, which depicts a woman waiting for her lover, deeply embedded in Jeju folklore.
Adjacent to Oedolgae Rock, this sandy beach offers swimming, water sports, and family-friendly activities during warmer months.
Follow designated paths along the Jungmun cliffs for panoramic ocean views and unique perspectives of Oedolgae from different vantage points.
Time your visit for late afternoon to witness the rock formation silhouetted against the Korean Strait during sunset.

food.detail: Try fresh grilled abalone, sea urchin, and local white fish at beachfront restaurants overlooking Oedolgae Rock.
Sample grilled or braised black pork, a regional specialty found at nearby restaurants, known for its tender texture and rich flavor.
Enjoy fresh local seaweed varieties served in soups or as side dishes at casual eateries near Jungmun Beach.
Warm up with steamed seafood (jjim) or spicy seafood stew (jjigae) at traditional restaurants in the Jungmun area.
Order seasonal raw fish platters at waterfront establishments, often prepared with locally caught fish from surrounding waters.

culture.detail: Oedolgae Rock holds deep meaning in Jeju culture as a symbol of fidelity and tragic love; visitors often leave wishes or small offerings.
Water levels and accessibility to lower viewpoints vary with tides; plan your visit timing accordingly for the best viewing experience.
The coastal paths and cliff edges can be slippery or uneven; sturdy, closed-toe shoes are recommended for safe navigation.
While photography is encouraged, be mindful of other visitors and maintain safe distance from cliff edges when positioning for shots.

places.name: Jeju Island
places.description: A volcanic island and premier vacation destination known for its natural beauty, beaches, and unique landscape including Hallasan mountain. It attracts millions annually for relaxation, outdoor activities, and its distinct cultural heritage.

activities.detail: Hike South Korea's highest peak at 1,950 meters with trails ranging from easy to challenging, offering crater lake views and alpine scenery.
Experience a rare waterfall that cascades directly onto a black sand beach, particularly spectacular during spring and autumn.
Walk through one of the world's longest lava tubes with impressive stalactites and natural formations created by volcanic activity.
Enjoy crystal-clear turquoise waters and soft white sand on this scenic beach ideal for swimming and water sports.
Learn about the island's famous female free-diving women and their centuries-old pearl and seafood harvesting tradition.
Stroll through a restored traditional Jeju village showcasing thatched-roof houses and authentic island architecture and lifestyle.
Browse quirky exhibitions of decorative teddy bears from around the world in a themed museum popular with families.

food.detail: Tender, flavorful grilled or braised pork from indigenous black pigs raised on the island, often served at specialized restaurants.
Fresh abalone harvested by haenyeo divers, served raw, grilled, or in soups as a premium local delicacy.
Traditional Jeju grain cakes made from barley and millet, historically eaten during lean seasons and now available as snacks.
Fresh sea urchin roe served over rice, showcasing Jeju's abundant seafood from surrounding waters.
Local noodle soup made with broth, vegetables, and thinly sliced meat, representing Jeju's comfort food traditions.

culture.detail: Acknowledge the skilled free-diving women as cultural ambassadors; visit their demonstrations or purchase directly from them to support this UNESCO-recognized heritage practice.
Always remove your footwear when entering homes, temples, or traditional Korean restaurants to show respect and follow local customs.
Swim only in designated beach areas and respect warning flags; understand that certain beaches have gender-specific bathing times due to haenyeo operations.

restaurants.name: Gujilpan
restaurants.description: Historic restaurant famous for gujilpan (nine-section dish) and traditional Korean cuisine prepared with local ingredients.

restaurants.name: Myeonok
restaurants.description: Beloved local institution serving excellent kalguksu and makguksu with housemade noodles and rich broths.

restaurants.name: Haenyeo House
restaurants.description: Family-run seafood restaurant featuring the catch of female divers (haenyeo) with fresh abalone and sea urchin.

restaurants.name: Black Pork Street (흑돼지거리)
restaurants.description: Legendary alley of grilled black pork restaurants, a Jeju specialty, with charcoal grills and authentic preparation.

restaurants.name: Saebyeol
restaurants.description: Casual haenyeo restaurant offering simple, fresh raw fish and seafood at reasonable prices with ocean views.

restaurants.name: Hanwoori
restaurants.description: Premium black pork establishment with high-quality cuts, attentive service, and traditional charcoal grilling.

restaurants.name: Kkoma Sikdang
restaurants.description: Unpretentious local spot serving hearty seafood stews and grilled fish near the haenyeo diving village.

restaurants.name: Jeju Hangwa
restaurants.description: Modern traditional sweets café offering Jeju-inspired hangwa, teas, and pastries in a stylish setting.

restaurants.name: Sushi Inoue
restaurants.description: Omakase-focused sushi restaurant with a Japanese chef using premium local Jeju seafood and imported ingredients.

restaurants.name: Myeongpa Sikdang
restaurants.description: Simple family restaurant specializing in spicy seafood stews made with daily fresh catch from local waters.

restaurants.name: Oscars Table
restaurants.description: Contemporary Korean restaurant showcasing seasonal ingredients and refined plating with a focus on local produce.
restaurants.mustOrder: Jeju Black Pork in Perilla Leaf

restaurants.name: Doota Jjim
restaurants.description: Bustling stew house where the owner personally selects daily fish to create rich, flavorful bokkeum jjim.

restaurants.name: Jeju Noodle
restaurants.description: Local favourite for delicate, thin kalguksu with seafood broth and homemade noodles at excellent value.

restaurants.name: Yeonnam Gomtang
restaurants.description: No-frills establishment famous for traditional cow bone broth (gomtang) simmered for hours, served with rice and side dishes.

regions.name: Seoul
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring (April-May) or autumn (September-October) for mild weather and clear skies.

regions.name: Incheon
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or autumn for comfortable coastal weather and beach exploration.

regions.name: Gyeonggi
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or fall for pleasant temperatures and agricultural scenery.

regions.name: Gangwon
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in summer for mountain hiking and winter for skiing at world-class resorts.

regions.name: North Chungcheong
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or autumn for temple visits and mountain scenery.

regions.name: South Chungcheong
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or autumn to explore coastal areas and cultural heritage sites.

regions.name: Daejeon
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit year-round for museums and technology attractions with best conditions in spring and autumn.

regions.name: North Jeolla
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or autumn for food festivals and traditional cultural experiences.

regions.name: South Jeolla
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or autumn for island exploration and fresh seafood.

regions.name: Gwangju
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring for flower festivals and year-round for art and cultural museums.

regions.name: North Gyeongsang
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in autumn for fall colors and cultural heritage exploration or spring for pleasant weather.

regions.name: Daegu
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or autumn to avoid summer heat and explore textile museums and temples.

regions.name: South Gyeongsang
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or autumn for coastal scenery and cultural festivals.

regions.name: Busan
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or autumn for pleasant coastal weather and beach activities.

regions.name: Ulsan
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or autumn for industrial heritage tours and nearby mountain attractions.

regions.name: Jeju
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or autumn for mild weather and clear views; winter for whale watching.

top10.name: Seoul
top10.description: South Korea's capital and primary entry point for most visitors, serving as the transport hub to reach Gangwon's attractions. While technically in a different province, it's essential for accessing trains and buses to Gangwon destinations.

top10.name: Jeju Island
top10.description: A volcanic island and premier vacation destination known for its natural beauty, beaches, and unique landscape including Hallasan mountain. It attracts millions annually for relaxation, outdoor activities, and its distinct cultural heritage.

top10.name: Busan
top10.description: South Korea's second-largest city and primary port, Busan is a vibrant coastal metropolis famous for its beaches, seafood markets, and modern skyline. Essential stops include Gamcheon Culture Village, Jagalchi Fish Market, and Haeundae Beach.

top10.name: Gyeongbokgung Palace
top10.description: The largest and most iconic of Seoul's five grand palaces, built in 1395 during the Joseon Dynasty. It showcases traditional Korean architecture, royal guards in ceremonial uniforms, and offers a deep dive into Korean royal history.

top10.name: Namsan Tower (Seoul Tower)
top10.description: An iconic 237-meter observation tower offering panoramic views of Seoul's skyline from multiple levels. Tourists visit for the spectacular vistas, dining options, and the tower's status as a symbol of Seoul.

top10.name: Incheon
top10.description: South Korea's third-largest city and a major international hub, home to Incheon International Airport and the gateway to Korea. The city blends modern development with historical significance, featuring diverse neighborhoods and cultural attractions.

top10.name: Myeongdong
top10.description: Seoul's premier shopping and entertainment district packed with international brands, street food vendors, and bustling crowds day and night. It's the heart of Korean pop culture and fashion where locals and tourists converge.

top10.name: Nami Island
top10.description: A scenic crescent-shaped island in the Han River near Seoul famous for its tree-lined paths, autumn foliage, and spring blossoms. It gained international fame from the Korean drama Winter Sonata and offers peaceful walks, cycling, and river views.

top10.name: Gwangju
top10.description: South Korea's fifth-largest city and cultural hub, known for its art museums, May 18 Democratic Uprising history, and vibrant food scene. The city is home to the Gwangju Biennale, Asia's leading contemporary art festival, and offers excellent museums and galleries.

top10.name: Bulguksa Temple
top10.description: One of Korea's most important Buddhist temples, Bulguksa is a masterpiece of Silla architecture built in 751 AD, featuring beautiful stone pagodas, bridges, and intricate wooden structures set against forested mountains. It's a UNESCO World Heritage site and essential to any visit to North Gyeongsang.

places-index.json.name: National Museum of Korea, Daegu Branch

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 40 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: Lunar New Year
festivals.description: Korea's most important traditional holiday, celebrated with family reunions, special foods, and ancestral rites. The entire nation observes this three-day public holiday.

festivals.name: Jinhae Cherry Blossom Festival
festivals.description: Korea's most famous cherry blossom celebration featuring over 350,000 cherry trees blooming around a scenic navy port. The festival transforms the city with illuminated trees and cultural events.

festivals.name: Boryeong Mud Festival
festivals.description: A unique beach festival where visitors cover themselves in mineral-rich mud from local clay, followed by games, concerts, and swimming. It's one of Asia's most eccentric and fun summer events.

festivals.name: Buddha's Birthday
festivals.description: A national holiday celebrating Buddha's birth with lantern festivals at temples nationwide and colorful street processions. Major temples hold special ceremonies and the streets are decorated with elaborate lanterns.

festivals.name: Jeju Fire Festival
festivals.description: A spring festival celebrating the burning of dry grass on Jeju's volcanic landscape, blending ancient tradition with modern performances. The festival includes folk games, fireworks, and cultural events.

festivals.name: Busan International Film Festival
festivals.description: Asia's largest film festival attracting international cineastes and celebrities to Korea's busiest port city. It showcases over 300 films from around the world and hosts major awards ceremonies.

festivals.name: Gwangju Kimchi Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating Korea's iconic fermented vegetable dish with cooking competitions, tastings, and cultural performances. Visitors can make their own kimchi and learn traditional preservation techniques.

festivals.name: Chuseok
festivals.description: Korea's second most important holiday, a harvest festival when families return home for ancestral rites and feasts. The entire nation observes this three-day public holiday with minimal business activity.

festivals.name: Seoul Lantern Festival
festivals.description: The Cheonggyecheon stream is transformed with thousands of artistic lanterns creating a magical atmosphere through the heart of the capital. The festival celebrates traditional and contemporary lantern art.

festivals.name: Andong Mask Dance Festival
festivals.description: One of Korea's most important traditional cultural festivals featuring ancient mask dances and rituals passed down for centuries. International performers join Korean artists in celebrating this UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage.

festivals.name: Daegu Chimac Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of Korea's beloved fried chicken and beer pairing with cooking contests, performances, and street food. The festival attracts hundreds of thousands of food lovers to taste premium chimac.

festivals.name: Icheon Ceramic Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating Korea's ceramics capital with exhibitions, workshops, and competitions showcasing traditional and contemporary pottery. Visitors can try their hand at the potter's wheel and purchase unique ceramic pieces.

festivals.name: Muju Firefly Festival
festivals.description: A summer festival celebrating the magical return of fireflies to the Muju valley with nighttime viewing tours and traditional cultural performances. Visitors experience the pristine ecology of this rural mountain region.

festivals.name: Incheon International Jazz Festival
festivals.description: One of Asia's premier jazz festivals bringing world-class musicians to the port city for a week of concerts and performances. The festival celebrates both traditional and contemporary jazz across multiple venues.

festivals.name: Namwon Chunhyang Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating the legendary love story of Chunhyang with traditional performances, costume parades, and reenactments of classical Korean theater. The festival keeps alive an important part of Korean literary heritage.

festivals.name: Gangreung Coffee Festival
festivals.description: Korea's premier coffee celebration in the mountainous region famous for specialty coffee cultivation. The festival features barista competitions, tastings, and cultural performances.

festivals.name: Jeonju International Film Festival
festivals.description: A prestigious film festival in Korea's cultural capital showcasing innovative independent and art cinema from around the world. The festival celebrates cinematic artistry and discovery.

festivals.name: Suncheon Bay Ecological Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating the wetlands ecosystem with nature walks, cultural performances, and environmental education in one of Asia's most beautiful bays. The festival promotes ecological conservation and sustainable tourism.

festivals.name: Korean Foundation Day
festivals.description: A national holiday commemorating the legendary founding of Gojoseon in 2333 BCE, Korea's first kingdom. The day features cultural celebrations and flag displays throughout the country.

festivals.name: Jindo Haenyeo Diving Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating the legendary female divers who harvest seafood by breath-holding, a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. Visitors can watch spectacular diving demonstrations and witness this ancient tradition.

festivals.name: Ulsan Whale Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating the return of migratory whales to Korean waters with performances, exhibitions, and marine education. The festival honors Korea's maritime heritage and conservation efforts.

festivals.name: Daejeon Science Festival
festivals.description: Korea's hub of science and technology celebrates innovation with interactive exhibits, demonstrations, and performances. The festival inspires public interest in STEM fields through engaging entertainment.

festivals.name: Gimhae Gaya Cultural Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating the ancient Gaya Kingdom with historical reenactments, traditional performances, and archaeological exhibitions. The festival preserves the legacy of this influential coastal civilization.

festivals.name: Hwacheon Sancheoneo Ice Festival
festivals.description: Korea's most famous winter festival on a frozen river where visitors can ice fish for trout and enjoy winter games. The festival attracts hundreds of thousands with its unique ice activities and traditional performances.

festivals.name: Chungju International Film Festival
festivals.description: A respected film festival showcasing quality cinema and emerging filmmakers in Korea's central region. The festival celebrates cinematic excellence and cultural exchange.

festivals.name: Memorial Day
festivals.description: A national holiday honoring those who died in wars and military service. Citizens observe moments of silence and visit memorials throughout the country.

festivals.name: Boshinggak New Year Bell Ringing
festivals.description: Ringing the historic Bosingak bell at midnight to welcome the new year is a Seoul tradition with thousands gathering for performances and celebrations. The ceremony symbolizes hope and renewal.

festivals.name: Boseong Green Tea Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating Korea's premier tea-growing region with picking demonstrations, tastings, and cultural performances among scenic emerald tea fields. Visitors experience traditional tea culture and can harvest their own leaves.

festivals.name: Wonju Paper Arts Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating traditional Korean paper (hanji) craftsmanship with exhibitions, workshops, and performances by master artisans. Visitors can create their own paper art and purchase handmade items.

festivals.name: Gwangju Biennale
festivals.description: One of Asia's most important contemporary art exhibitions showcasing innovative works and addressing global themes. The biennale elevates Gwangju as a center for contemporary Asian art.

festivals.name: Liberation Day
festivals.description: A national holiday commemorating Korea's liberation from Japanese colonial rule in 1945. The day features flag displays, patriotic ceremonies, and cultural celebrations.

festivals.name: Pohang Light Festival
festivals.description: Transforming a coastal industrial city with spectacular light installations and performances creating a magical winter atmosphere. The festival celebrates creativity and innovation through light art.

festivals.name: Jeonju Hanok Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating Korea's largest traditional hanok village with street performances, costume experiences, and cultural activities. Visitors immerse themselves in historical atmosphere and traditional arts.

festivals.name: Damyang Bamboo Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating bamboo culture in Korea's premier bamboo forest with performances, crafts, and food made from bamboo. Visitors can walk through the scenic forest and learn about traditional bamboo uses.

festivals.name: Seoul Jazz Festival
festivals.description: Korea's capital celebrates world-class jazz with performances by international and Korean artists across multiple venues. The festival brings sophisticated music and cultural exchange to central Seoul.

festivals.name: Giheung Strawberry Festival
festivals.description: Celebrating Korea's strawberry season with tastings, picking experiences, and cultural performances. The festival showcases the region's agricultural heritage and winter harvest.

festivals.name: Children's Day
festivals.description: A national holiday celebrating children with special events, performances, and festivities throughout the country. Parks and venues offer free or discounted admission for families.

festivals.name: Myeongdong Street Festival
festivals.description: Transforming Seoul's busy shopping district with street performances, flash mobs, and cultural celebrations. The festival brings artistic energy to one of Korea's most vibrant commercial areas.

festivals.name: Incheon Pentaport Rock Festival
festivals.description: Korea's premier rock and indie music festival attracting international and domestic bands to the port city. The festival showcases diverse musical styles and alternative culture.

festivals.name: Hangeul Day
festivals.description: A national holiday celebrating the invention of Hangeul, Korea's alphabet, in 1443. The day features cultural events highlighting Korean language and literary heritage.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of South Korea
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Paleolithic and Neolithic Korea
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers gradually transitioned to farming in the Korean peninsula.
items.text: The earliest humans arrived on the Korean peninsula during the Paleolithic era, leaving behind stone tools and cave paintings. By the Neolithic period, communities developed agriculture, pottery, and dolmen burial structures that still dot the landscape today. These early inhabitants laid the foundation for Bronze Age cultures that would emerge in subsequent millennia. The transition from nomadic hunting to settled farming marked the beginning of Korean civilization's long arc.

items.label: Gojoseon
items.summary: Korea's legendary first kingdom established a Bronze Age civilization in Manchuria and the peninsula.
items.text: According to traditional accounts, Dangun founded Gojoseon in 2333 BC, making it one of the world's oldest kingdoms, though modern historians debate the exact chronology. This kingdom developed a sophisticated bronze culture and established trade networks across East Asia. Gojoseon absorbed multiple waves of migration and developed its own unique governmental systems and spiritual traditions. The kingdom's eventual fall to Chinese Han forces in 108 BC marked the beginning of Chinese influence on the peninsula that would last for centuries.

items.label: Three Kingdoms Era
items.summary: Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla competed for dominance while Chinese cultural influences flooded the peninsula.
items.text: After Gojoseon's collapse, three major kingdoms emerged: Goguryeo in the north, Baekje in the southwest, and Silla in the southeast, each vying for supremacy. Buddhism arrived during this period and became deeply woven into Korean culture, inspiring magnificent temple architecture and artistic traditions. Chinese administrative systems, writing, and philosophies transformed Korean governance, though each kingdom maintained distinct identities and fierce independence. Constant warfare between the kingdoms created an era of cultural dynamism but also instability, as each power sought regional hegemony.

items.label: Unified Silla
items.summary: Silla conquered its rivals and unified the peninsula, ushering in a golden age of Buddhist culture.
items.text: Silla's military genius and strategic alliances allowed it to absorb Baekje in 660 and Goguryeo in 668, achieving the peninsula's first unified kingdom. Under rulers like Queen Sondok and King Seongdeok, Silla experienced a cultural renaissance that produced exquisite Buddhist temples, advanced astronomical observatories, and thriving arts. The kingdom adopted Tang Chinese administrative models while maintaining Korean cultural identity, creating a sophisticated court society. Silla's prosperity and stability made it a beacon of East Asian civilization, though by the 9th century, regional warlords began fragmenting its authority.

items.label: Goryeo Dynasty
items.summary: A new dynasty reunified Korea and gave the peninsula its modern name through sophisticated governance and artistic achievement.
items.text: Wang Geon founded Goryeo after absorbing the fragmented remains of Silla, restoring unity and establishing a dynasty that would last nearly five centuries. The name Korea itself derives from Goryeo, and the kingdom became renowned for its celadon pottery, Buddhist sculpture, and the invention of woodblock printing and metal movable type. Goryeo developed a civil service examination system based on Confucian ideals, creating a educated bureaucratic class that managed the kingdom with remarkable sophistication. The dynasty weathered Mongol invasions and internal strife, but gradually weakened as Confucianism challenged Buddhism's cultural dominance and military juntas destabilized governance.

items.label: Joseon Dynasty
items.summary: A new Confucian kingdom replaced Buddhism with state ideology and created a cultural golden age that defined Korean civilization for five centuries.
items.text: General Yi Seong-gye founded Joseon after overthrowing the weakened Goryeo, inaugurating the peninsula's longest-lasting dynasty and establishing Confucianism as the state philosophy. Under visionary rulers like Sejong the Great, Joseon achieved remarkable innovations including the Korean alphabet Hangul, advanced military technology, and astronomical breakthroughs. The society became rigidly hierarchical with a yangban aristocratic class dominating politics and culture, while women's rights deteriorated and the caste system hardened. Despite the Imjin War (1592–1598) against Japanese invader Toyotomi Hideyoshi and subsequent Manchu invasions, Joseon recovered and experienced a cultural flowering in the 17th and 18th centuries.

items.label: Joseon Renaissance
items.summary: The dynasty's final prosperous era produced remarkable intellectual movements and practical innovations before Western pressure destabilized the kingdom.
items.text: The 18th century marked Joseon's cultural and intellectual peak, with scholars pursuing practical learning that challenged rigid Neo-Confucian orthodoxy and inspired technological advancement. King Jeongjo patronized the Suwon Fortress and sponsored extensive historical compilations, while merchants and proto-capitalists began challenging the yangban monopoly on wealth and status. Catholic Christianity quietly spread among intellectuals and commoners alike, offering alternative spiritual paths to the established order. However, this flourishing period coincided with growing Western pressure on East Asia, internal factional conflicts, and economic strain that would eventually overwhelm the aging dynasty.

items.label: Forced Opening and Modernization
items.summary: Japan forced Korea open to the modern world and gradually colonized the peninsula through military and economic domination.
items.text: Japan's military pressure compelled Korea to sign the Ganghwa Treaty in 1876, ending centuries of isolation and opening ports to foreign trade and influence. Modernization initiatives clashed with conservative Confucian elites, producing civil unrest including the Donghak Peasant Revolt and triggering Japanese intervention in Korean affairs. The Russo-Japanese War (1904–1905) saw Korea become a Japanese protectorate, with the peninsula's sovereignty systematically stripped away through unequal treaties and military occupation. By 1910, Japan formally annexed Korea, ending the Joseon Dynasty and beginning 35 years of colonial rule marked by cultural suppression and economic exploitation.

items.label: Japanese Colonial Period
items.summary: Japan's brutal colonization modernized Korea's infrastructure while crushing Korean identity and independence aspirations.
items.text: Japanese colonial administrators built railroads, factories, and modern cities while systematically erasing Korean culture through forced assimilation policies including mandatory Japanese language education. The March 1st Movement of 1919 saw mass protests against occupation, brutally suppressed but inspiring nationalist movements that would persist throughout the colonial period. Korean intellectuals, artists, and revolutionaries pursued independence through armed resistance, cultural preservation, and exile movements, with many finding refuge in Manchuria and Shanghai. World War II's end in 1945 finally liberated Korea, but the peninsula immediately faced division by Soviet and American forces, thwarting dreams of immediate reunification.

items.label: Division and the Korean War
items.summary: Korea split into two hostile nations at the 38th parallel, then plunged into a devastating war that killed millions and cemented division.
items.text: Soviet and American military forces divided Korea at the 38th parallel in 1945, establishing separate zones of occupation that quickly hardened into rival states with incompatible ideologies. The Democratic People's Republic of Korea emerged under communist leader Kim Il-sung in the north, while the Republic of Korea formed in the south under authoritarian Syngman Rhee. On June 25, 1950, North Korea invaded the south, triggering the Korean War as United Nations forces, led by the United States, clashed with North Korean and Chinese armies. The three-year conflict killed approximately three million people, devastated both nations, and ended with an armistice that established a demilitarized zone, leaving Korea permanently divided into two separate countries.

items.label: Post-War Division and South Korea's Rise
items.summary: South Korea rebuilt from ashes through authoritarian industrialization, transforming from poor agrarian nation to economic powerhouse.
items.text: After the armistice, South Korea lay in ruins with a devastated economy and traumatized population, yet visionary leaders pursued rapid industrialization through government-directed development. Military strongman Park Chung-hee (1963–1979) implemented five-year economic plans, built export-driven manufacturing, and constructed infrastructure like the Seoul-Busan highway, sacrificing democratic freedoms for economic growth. By the 1970s and 1980s, South Korea's chaebol conglomerates like Samsung and Hyundai achieved global prominence, transforming the nation into an industrial tiger and dramatically raising living standards. This period of authoritarianism and repression also produced vibrant cultural resistance, labor movements, and Christian faith communities that would ultimately demand democratic reform.

items.label: Democratization and Global Emergence
items.summary: South Korea transitioned to democracy, hosted the Olympic Games, and established itself as a cultural and technological leader.
items.text: The June Democratic Struggle of 1987 forced the military government to accept direct presidential elections, ending decades of authoritarianism and initiating South Korea's democratic transformation. The 1988 Seoul Olympics showcased the nation's modernization to the world while massive labor strikes and civil rights movements reshaped society from below. South Korea's entertainment and popular culture industries began capturing global audiences through cinema, music, and television dramas, establishing soft power that complemented its technological prowess. The 1997 Asian financial crisis temporarily shocked the economy but spurred recovery through corporate restructuring and further globalization.

items.label: Contemporary South Korea
items.summary: South Korea became a global technology and culture powerhouse while navigating ongoing tensions with the North and digital transformation.
items.text: The 21st century saw South Korea dominate global technology markets through companies like Samsung, LG, and SK Hynix, while also leading the digital revolution with world-class internet infrastructure and mobile innovation. Korean popular culture achieved unprecedented global dominance through the Korean Wave, with BTS, BLACKPINK, Squid Game, and Parasite captivating international audiences and generating massive cultural influence. South Korea balanced remarkable economic achievements with persistent challenges including gender inequality, intense education competition, and ongoing military tensions with North Korea across the demilitarized zone. Today South Korea stands as a fully developed democracy and high-income nation, yet remains technically at war with the North, with periodic diplomatic initiatives offering tentative hope for eventual reconciliation.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in South Korea — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Cafés are social hubs, not just for coffee
items.text: South Koreans spend hours in cafés studying, working, or socializing—it's normal to nurse one coffee for 3+ hours. Most cafés have free WiFi and outlets, making them de facto co-working spaces.

items.label: Americano [아메리카노]
items.text: The default coffee order, usually around 3,000-5,000 won. It's stronger and cheaper than lattes, making it the everyday choice for locals.

items.label: Convenience store coffee is surprisingly good
items.text: GS25, CU, and Emart24 sell decent drip coffee for 1,500-2,500 won—perfectly serviceable if you're on a budget or in a rush.

items.label: Hongdae & Gangnam for trendy cafés
items.text: These neighborhoods have Instagram-worthy independent cafés with specialty coffee, but expect to pay 6,000-10,000 won per drink. Tourist areas near palaces charge premiums.

items.label: Bibimbap [비빔밥]
items.text: Mixed rice topped with vegetables, meat, egg, and spicy gochujang sauce—stir it all together before eating. It's healthy, filling, and available everywhere from street stalls to restaurants.

items.label: Korean BBQ [한우구이]
items.text: Grill meat at your table—beef (hanwoo) is premium but pork (dwaeji) is affordable and equally satisfying. Always order side dishes (banchan) which come free and include kimchi, pickled vegetables, and more.

items.label: Kimchi Jjigae [김치찌개]
items.text: Spicy kimchi stew with pork, tofu, and vegetables—deeply comforting and cheap (8,000-12,000 won). Perfect for cold days and a true taste of home cooking.

items.label: Tteokbokki [떡볶이]
items.text: Chewy rice cakes in spicy red sauce with fish cakes and vegetables—a beloved street food for 4,000-6,000 won. Highly addictive and often customizable with extra toppings.

items.label: Jjamppong [짬뽕]
items.text: Spicy seafood noodle soup bursting with shrimp, squid, and mussels—order with caution if you can't handle heat. Usually 12,000-15,000 won and worth every penny.

items.label: Kalguksu [칼국수]
items.text: Hand-cut noodles in a savory broth with vegetables and seafood—lighter than jjamppong but equally satisfying. A comforting lunch dish around 8,000-10,000 won.

items.label: Hotteok [호떡]
items.text: Sweet Korean pancake filled with brown sugar, cinnamon, and sometimes nuts—sold at street carts for 3,000-4,000 won. Best eaten warm and perfect as a snack or dessert.

items.label: Pojangmacha [포장마차] street tents
items.text: Informal outdoor food stalls serving tteokbokki, hotteok, and street snacks—super cheap (3,000-8,000 won) and where locals actually eat. Most active in evenings and near subway stations.

items.label: Sikdangs [식당] for authentic meals
items.text: No-frills Korean restaurants with plastic chairs and laminated menus—expect honest, delicious food at fair prices (8,000-15,000 won). These are where Korean families eat, not tourists.

items.label: Department store food courts
items.text: Higher prices (12,000-20,000 won) but very clean and reliable; great if you're overwhelmed by options or need a quick sit-down meal without language barriers.

items.label: Avoid restaurants in major tourist zones
items.text: Myeongdong, Insadong, and palace areas inflate prices by 30-50% and serve diluted versions of authentic dishes. Venture 2-3 blocks away for better value.

items.label: Night markets like Namdaemun
items.text: Bustling traditional markets with hundreds of food stalls—perfect for sampling multiple dishes cheaply and seeing how locals shop and eat.

items.label: Most menus have photos—use them
items.text: Point at pictures or use your phone's camera translator app (Papago or Google Translate) to photograph menu text. English menus often mistranslate dish names, so photos are more reliable.

items.label: Spice levels matter—ask before ordering
items.text: If you can't handle heat, explicitly ask '맵지 않게 해주세요' (maep-ji an-ke hae-ju-se-yo, 'not spicy please'). Many Korean dishes default to fiery, and there's no shame in requesting mild.

items.label: Banchan [반찬] are free side dishes
items.text: Kimchi, pickled vegetables, and other sides arrive automatically—it's part of the meal, not something to order. You can ask for refills of popular ones.

items.label: Portion sizes are generous
items.text: One dish often feeds 1.5-2 people, especially soups and stews. Share or expect leftovers—restaurants freely pack extras to-go (no judgment).

items.label: Always check prices before ordering
items.text: Korean restaurants rarely have menu English but prices are always in won—watch out for 'special' or premium versions costing 2-3x more. High-end hanwoo beef can easily be 50,000+ won per serving.

items.label: Soju [소주]
items.text: Clear, high-proof Korean spirit (16-20% ABV)—cheap, smooth, and the default drink for social occasions. Expect 2,500-5,000 won per bottle at bars; it's often paired with fried food (chimaek culture).

items.label: Beer [맥주] and Soju bombs [소맥]
items.text: Local beers like Cass and Hite are crisp and affordable (4,000-6,000 won). Mixing beer and soju ('soju bomb') is a popular drinking game—fun but dangerous for your head the next day.

items.label: Makgeolli [막걸리]
items.text: Sweet, milky rice wine (6-8% ABV) traditionally served in bowls—nostalgic and less harsh than soju. Trendy makgeolli bars have emerged, but street pojangmacha versions are authentic and cheaper.

items.label: Pojangmacha for drinking culture
items.text: Street tent bars serve soju and beer with fried snacks (tteokbokki, hotteok, fried chicken)—this is where Koreans drink and socialize. Expect 15,000-30,000 won per person for drinks and food.

items.label: Chimaek [치맥] is fried chicken + beer
items.text: A beloved pairing: crispy, spicy fried chicken (치킨) with cold beer (맥주). Restaurants and pojangmacha focus on this—it's casual, affordable (15,000-25,000 won), and authentically Korean.

items.label: Respectful drinking customs matter
items.text: When older people pour drinks, accept with both hands and turn slightly away when drinking. Never pour your own drink if others are present—it's considered rude. Always pour for others first.

items.label: Wait for the eldest person to start eating
items.text: In formal settings or with Korean families, wait for the oldest person to pick up their spoon before you start. This shows respect and is deeply ingrained in Korean culture.

items.label: Use spoons for rice and soup, chopsticks for side dishes
items.text: Korean etiquette specifies spoons for rice (not chopsticks), chopsticks for banchan and meat. Metal utensils are traditional and standard in most restaurants.

items.label: Slurping noodles is acceptable and even encouraged
items.text: Unlike some cultures, loud slurping shows you're enjoying the food. It's normal behavior in Korean restaurants.

items.label: Never pour your own drink—pour for others instead
items.text: At meals and drinks, hold the bottle with both hands and offer to pour for others first. They'll reciprocate, creating a social rhythm. Pouring your own is seen as selfish.

items.label: Tipping is not customary and not expected
items.text: Unlike the US, tipping in South Korea is rare and can even be insulting. Prices shown are final, and service charges are sometimes included in bills at upscale restaurants.

items.label: Finish your rice as a sign of respect
items.text: Leaving rice uneaten is considered wasteful and disrespectful to the cook, especially in traditional or family settings. Always finish or ask for a small portion if you can't eat much.

items.label: Payment etiquette: one person pays, or split evenly
items.text: It's common for the oldest or most senior person to pay, or everyone splits the bill exactly (no itemizing). Offering to pay individually is rare and can create awkwardness.

items.label: Learn basic food phrases
items.text: Key phrases: '맛있어요!' (mat-iseo-yo, 'delicious!'), '물 주세요' (mul ju-se-yo, 'water please'), '계산서 주세요' (gye-san-seo ju-se-yo, 'bill please'). Koreans appreciate effort and are forgiving of mistakes.

items.label: Download a translation app
items.text: Papago (Korean-native) or Google Translate's camera feature are lifesavers for reading menus and understanding prices. Most restaurants lack English, so these tools are essential.

items.label: Allergen warnings: tell staff immediately
items.text: If you have allergies, write them down or use your phone to show them clearly. Many dishes contain hidden sesame, shellfish, or soy, so don't assume—always confirm.

items.label: Cash is still king at street food and small restaurants
items.text: While cards are accepted everywhere, many pojangmacha and small sikdangs are cash-only. Keep 10,000-50,000 won notes handy.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around South Korea.

items.label: KTX is fastest
items.text: Korea Train eXpress (KTX) connects major cities like Seoul, Busan, and Gwangju in 2–5 hours. Book via Korail.com or the Korail app; seats fill quickly during weekends and holidays.

items.label: Cheaper alternatives
items.text: Mugunghwa and Seemaul trains cost 30–50% less than KTX but take longer. Use these for budget travel between secondary cities.
Strikes occasionally disrupt service, especially in autumn. Check Korail's website or news before booking long trips.

items.label: No eating on KTX
items.text: Outside the dining car, eating strong-smelling food is prohibited. Beverages are allowed at your seat.

items.label: Reserve in advance
items.text: Book 30 days ahead online for the best prices and seat selection, especially for peak travel dates.

items.label: Metro is the fastest
items.text: Seoul, Busan, and Daegu have extensive subway systems with frequent trains (2–8 minute intervals). Signs in English and Korean make navigation straightforward.

items.label: Use a transit card
items.text: Buy a rechargeable T-money or Cashbee card at convenience stores (GS25, CU, Emart24) for seamless metro and bus payment. A single journey in Seoul costs ₩1,250–₩2,450.
Crowded during rush hours (7–9am, 5–7pm). Trains can be extremely packed, especially in Seoul; women-only cars exist but may still be crowded.

items.label: Etiquette rules
items.text: Do not eat, drink (except water), or apply makeup on trains. Speaking loudly on phones is considered rude and may draw stares.

items.label: Buses for longer routes
items.text: City buses (often with numbers 100–999) cover areas metro doesn't reach. Airport buses are a cost-effective option for getting to/from airports.

items.label: Naver Map app
items.text: Use Naver Map or Kakao Map to navigate metro and bus routes in real time. Both show departure times, transfers, and fares in English.

items.label: Regular taxis are cheap
items.text: Base fare is ₩3,800 (Seoul), with ₩100 per 142 meters or 35 seconds of waiting. They're abundant and a quick way to reach non-metro areas.
Some drivers refuse non-Korean passengers or don't know English. Have your destination written in Korean or use a map app to show the driver.

items.label: Premium options
items.text: Kakao Taxi and Naver Taxi apps let you book taxis in advance and see fares upfront. Uber is unavailable, but Kakao Taxi's premium service (Kakao Black) offers higher-end vehicles.

items.label: Night surcharge
items.text: After midnight, taxis add a 20% surcharge. Prices can spike significantly during late-night hours.

items.label: Always get a receipt
items.text: Request a receipt (영수증) for your records, especially if paying by card, to avoid disputes or scams.

items.label: International Driving Permit required
items.text: You must carry an International Driving Permit (IDP) alongside your home country's license. Korean police conduct frequent checks and fines for missing documentation are steep.

items.label: Speed limits & enforcement
items.text: Urban speed limits are 50–60 km/h, highways 100–110 km/h. Speeding fines start at ₩100,000 and can exceed ₩300,000 for major violations. Speed cameras are widespread.

items.label: Aggressive driving culture
items.text: Korean drivers are impatient and lane-changing is frequent. Stay defensive and avoid driving at night if unfamiliar with local habits.

items.label: Alcohol limits & fines
items.text: Zero-tolerance policy: any detectable alcohol results in a mandatory ₩1 million fine, license suspension, and possible jail time. Use taxis or public transport after drinking.

items.label: Rental agencies
items.text: Major companies like Lotte Car Rental, Avis, and Hertz operate at airports. Daily rates average ₩50,000–₩100,000. Fuel is expensive (₩1,600–₩1,700 per liter).
Parking fines are common and expensive (₩20,000–₩100,000+). Many central areas use complex permit systems; use parking apps like ParkNow to avoid violations.

items.label: Incheon (ICN) main hub
items.text: Incheon International Airport, 48 km west of Seoul, handles most international flights. It's modern, efficient, and has excellent ground transport links.

items.label: Airport rail links
items.text: The AREX (Airport Rail eXpress) express train reaches Seoul Station in 43 minutes for ₩9,000. Local trains take 60 minutes but cost ₩4,250 and serve more stations.

items.label: Airport buses
items.text: Limousine buses (공항리무진) run to major hotels and areas; fares are ₩10,000–₩25,000. City buses (100–600 series) are cheapest (₩2,500–₩10,000) but slower.

items.label: Gimpo (GMP) closer to Seoul
items.text: Gimpo International Airport, 18 km west, serves mainly regional and domestic flights. It's closer to central Seoul (20 minutes by rail) than Incheon.
Airport taxis cost ₩60,000–₩80,000 to central Seoul plus tolls. Only use official metered taxis or pre-booked services to avoid scams.

items.label: Island access
items.text: Ferries connect mainland to islands like Jeju (high-speed ferries: 3–3.5 hours), Namhae, and Tongyeong. Operators include Korea Ferry and Jeju Shipping.

items.label: Book in advance
items.text: Summer and weekends fill quickly. Reserve at ferry company websites or through Naver/Kakao Map. Prices vary ₩15,000–₩60,000 depending on route and vessel.
Rough seas can cause cancellations and refunds, especially in winter. Check weather and sea conditions before booking.

items.label: Luggage & vehicle fees
items.text: Large luggage incurs additional charges (₩5,000–₩10,000 per piece). Bringing a car costs significantly more; book separately.

items.label: T-money card best option
items.text: A rechargeable transit card (T-money or Cashbee) works across subways, buses, and taxis nationwide. Buy at convenience stores for ₩2,500 and load funds as needed.

items.label: Day passes for tourists
items.text: Seoul offers a 1-day metro pass (₩8,500) and combined metro/bus pass (₩10,800). Other cities like Busan have similar options; check local tourism sites.

items.label: Rail pass for long trips
items.text: The Korea Rail Pass (foreign tourists only) offers unlimited KTX/Mugunghwa travel for 3–7 consecutive days (₩65,000–₩215,000). Buy before arrival or at tourist centers.

items.label: Contactless payment limits
items.text: Some older ticket machines don't accept foreign credit cards; have cash (₩10,000+ notes) or use your transit card instead.

items.label: Convenience store purchases
items.text: GS25, CU, and Emart24 sell single-journey tickets and transit cards. Staff speak minimal English, but ticketing is straightforward and machines have English menus.

items.label: SIM cards at airports
items.text: Buy prepaid SIM cards from SK Telecom, KT, or LG U+ at airport convenience stores or info desks. Starter packages (₩20,000–₩50,000) include data and calls; activation is instant.

items.label: Cheap data plans
items.text: Monthly prepaid plans offer unlimited data for ₩30,000–₩50,000. Day passes (₩3,000–₩7,000) suit short stays. Speed is excellent (LTE/5G) nationwide.

items.label: WiFi widely available
items.text: Free WiFi (공공 와이파이) is available at cafes, malls, metro stations, and hotels. Connect via 'Seoul_WiFi' (Seoul) or similar city networks using your phone number.
Using a phone while driving incurs a ₩120,000+ fine and license deduction. Mount devices safely or use hands-free systems.

items.label: Essential apps
items.text: Download Naver Map, Kakao Map, Kakao Talk (messaging), and Papago (translation) before arrival. These are used by locals and work offline for maps.
